synopsis: The Profit Zone argues that market share alone no longer determines corporate success, and that companies must redesign their business models around the areas where the greatest customer value—and thus profit—can be captured. Slywotzky and Morrison examine how leading companies identify and dominate their industries' shifting profit zones while competitors remain trapped in low-margin no-profit territory.

Tim Congdon's book defends and updates the quantity theory of money, arguing that excessive growth in the money supply caused the high inflation of 2022 and early 2023—an outcome he publicly predicted from late 2020 when central banks dismissed the risk. The book explains in accessible but rigorous terms why the quantity theory retains analytical power that monetary policymakers abandoned to their cost.

sources.title: The Quantity Theory of Money at Barnes & Noble

synopsis: Daniel Yergin surveys the global quest for energy across oil, natural gas, renewables, and electricity, tracing how competing energy systems shape geopolitics, economic power, and climate policy. The book examines the forces driving the transition from fossil fuels to new energy sources and the security dilemmas nations face as they navigate that shift.

Michael Hollister is convicted of murder and takes his own life in a 1977 jail cell, only to wake in his eight-year-old body in 1966 with all memories intact, determined to confront the abusive father whose violence shaped his violent future. The novel follows his attempt to prevent the greatest wrong of his childhood without repeating the cycle that destroyed his original life.

sources.title: The Redemption of Michael Hollister at Fantastic Fiction

synopsis: Robert Gordon contends that the century between 1870 and 1970 was a singular period of transformation in American living standards, driven by electricity, indoor plumbing, motor vehicles, and modern medicine—improvements of a scale that cannot be replicated. He argues that the economy since 1970 faces structural headwinds including rising inequality, educational stagnation, and demographic change that will suppress future growth.

sources.title: The Rise and Fall of American Growth – Princeton University Press

synopsis: Edmund Morris traces Theodore Roosevelt's life from a sickly, asthmatic childhood through his careers as naturalist, writer, New York police commissioner, assistant secretary of the Navy, and Rough Rider colonel, showing how these roles fused into the force that propelled him to the presidency at forty-two. The biography covers the years 1858 to 1901 and portrays Roosevelt's deliberate self-transformation as central to his political rise.

sources.title: The Rise of Theodore Roosevelt – Penguin Random House

synopsis: The River of Consciousness collects ten essays in which Oliver Sacks investigates fundamental questions about how the mind works, including the nature of creativity, memory, time perception, and consciousness itself, drawing on Darwin, Freud, and William James alongside neuroscientific research. The collection was completed by Sacks shortly before his death and represents his most sustained engagement with the ideas that defined his intellectual life.

sources.title: The River of Consciousness – Penguin Random House

synopsis: The Roll Model presents Jill Miller's self-massage system using therapy balls to relieve pain, improve mobility, and increase body awareness through techniques she calls the Roll Model Method. The book combines anatomical explanations, step-by-step rolling sequences from head to toe, and case studies illustrating how the approach addresses chronic pain and movement restrictions.

sources.title: The Roll Model at Barnes & Noble

synopsis: Bill Walsh outlines the leadership philosophy he applied while transforming the San Francisco 49ers from the NFL's worst franchise into a dynasty, centering on the idea that excellence in organizational process—what he calls the Standard of Performance—produces winning results rather than the other way around. The book draws on Walsh's career to illustrate principles about building culture, teaching, and maintaining standards under pressure.

sources.title: The Score Takes Care of Itself – Penguin Random House

synopsis: Erik Brynjolfsson and Andrew McAfee argue that digital technology is driving a second machine age in which computers begin to automate cognitive tasks as the industrial revolution automated physical ones, generating enormous economic bounty while simultaneously disrupting labor markets and widening inequality. The book examines which skills and policies will allow workers and societies to share the gains from accelerating technological progress.

sources.title: The Second Machine Age – W. W. Norton & Company

synopsis: Aiden Bishop wakes at an isolated English country house with no memory of who he is, and must solve the murder of Evelyn Hardcastle before midnight—each time the day resets he inhabits a different guest's body, seeing events from a new angle. At least two rivals are racing to solve the same crime, and only one player will escape Blackheath.

sources.title: The Seven Deaths of Evelyn Hardcastle – Wikipedia

synopsis: Henk Tennekes explores the aerodynamic principles shared by insects, birds, and aircraft, showing that creatures as different as gnats and jumbo jets obey the same physical laws governing wing size, energy consumption, and lift. The revised edition adds analysis of bar-tailed godwit migration, the convergence of modern jetliners, and new treatment of drag and trailing vortices.

sources.title: The Simple Science of Flight – MIT Press

synopsis: Ray Kurzweil argues that accelerating technological progress—driven by the exponential growth of computing power—will lead by 2029 to artificial intelligence that surpasses human intelligence in all domains, and by 2045 to a Singularity in which humans and machines merge. The book presents a framework of overlapping technological revolutions in genetics, nanotechnology, and robotics as the pathway to this transformation.

sources.title: The Singularity Is Near – Official Site

synopsis: Kurzweil revisits and updates the predictions of his 2005 book, tracking a decade of breakthroughs in AI, machine learning, and biotechnology that he argues confirm the trajectory toward a 2045 Singularity where human and machine intelligence converge. The book projects that nanobots will connect the human neocortex to cloud-based AI by the 2030s, expanding cognition and eventually eliminating disease and death.

sources.title: The Singularity Is Nearer – Penguin Random House

synopsis: Bethany McLean and Peter Elkind reconstruct the rise and collapse of Enron, tracing how executives Ken Lay, Jeff Skilling, and Andy Fastow used accounting manipulation, off-balance-sheet entities, and a culture of arrogance to conceal the company's true condition from investors and regulators. The book draws on internal documents and interviews to explain how one of America's largest corporations became one of its greatest frauds.

sources.title: The Smartest Guys in the Room – Penguin Random House

synopsis: Gregory Clark uses surname tracking across more than twenty societies and eight centuries to argue that social mobility rates are far lower and more persistent than standard estimates suggest, remaining largely constant regardless of policy or regime. The findings challenge the assumption that education reform or redistribution can meaningfully accelerate movement up and down the social ladder.

sources.title: The Son Also Rises – Princeton University Press

synopsis: Davidson and Rees-Mogg forecast that the information age will erode nation-state power by making it impossible for governments to tax and coerce mobile individuals and capital, producing what they call a fourth stage of human society in which sovereign individuals transact outside traditional political structures. The book outlines financial strategies for adapting to this transition and examines consequences for democracy and warfare.

Cara works as a multiverse traverser whose parallel selves have died on 372 of the known parallel Earths, since travel requires the traveler to already be dead at the destination—a condition that makes her uniquely employable. When a surviving counterpart is killed under suspicious circumstances, she uncovers a conspiracy that forces her to confront questions of identity and who gets to survive.

sources.title: The Space Between Worlds – Penguin Random House

synopsis: John Newhouse examines the high-stakes competitive dynamics of the commercial airliner industry, focusing on the rivalries among Boeing, Lockheed, McDonnell Douglas, and the newly formed Airbus Industrie as they gamble enormous sums on each new aircraft program. The book traces the roles of governments, international banks, and airline executives in shaping an industry where a single wrong bet can destroy a company.
